reasoning: Katie Ledecky recently lowered her own long-course 800m freestyle world record to 8:04.12 in May 2025. She also just won the event at the 2025 World Aquatics Championships in August with a time of 8:05.62, reinforcing her immense dominance. The nearest competitors in history (like Summer McIntosh and Ariarne Titmus) are around 7-9 seconds slower than her record time. Furthermore, the period between September 2025 and February 2026 represents the traditional off-season for major long-course swimming, focusing more on short-course (25m) events like the World Cup and Short Course World Championships. For Ledecky to lose the record, someone else would have to achieve an unprecedented drop in record time during an off-season long-course meet, or Ledecky would have to be retroactively disqualified. Both scenarios are exceedingly unlikely, keeping her record almost unassailable through early 2026. The slight decrease in probabilities simply reflects standard time discounting for uncertainty over a 6-month horizon.
